answer.rationale: Pan-canonical lexical pattern: John 1:1-2 imperfect ἦν (G1510, continuous past existence) places the Logos with God and as God before all creation; John 1:18 names the Son ὁ ὢν εἰς τὸν κόλπον τοῦ πατρός (present participle of eimi, the one who is in the bosom of the Father, ongoing state); John 8:58 contrasts aorist γενέσθαι (G1096, came into being) of Abraham with present ἐγὼ εἰμί (G1510) of the Son; John 17:5 has the Son requesting glory shared παρὰ σοί πρὸ τοῦ τὸν κόσμον εἶναι (with you before the world existed); Col 1:15-17 names the Son πρωτότοκος (G4416, rank not chronology, cf. Ps 89:27 LXX where David the youngest is named firstborn) πρὸ πάντων (before all things); Heb 1:2-3 makes the Son the agent δι' οὗ ἐποίησεν τοὺς αἰῶνας (through whom he made the worlds), so the Son cannot be among created things; Phil 2:6 ἐν μορφῇ θεοῦ ὑπάρχων (present participle of huparcho, continuously existing in the form of God). Heb 1:5 quoting Ps 2:7 (σήμερον γεγέννηκά σε) is a royal coronation-enthronement text in its psalm context, applied to the Son's installation as messianic king, not the origin of his Sonship.
answer.notes: Cult-marker line is nuanced. The hard-denial form (the Son was created, had a beginning, became Son only at incarnation or baptism) is cult-grade when carried by movements that read it into the totality of their christology: Arianism in its historic form, Jehovah's Witnesses (Christ as the created angel Michael), Christadelphians (Christ as created Son who began at the incarnation), Mormonism (the Son as a separate created God, spiritually born of the Father in pre-mortal life), Iglesia ni Cristo (Christ as mere man). These deny eternal Sonship and with it the eternal Father-Son relation that defines the gospel mediator structure. A softer form is the incarnational-Sonship view briefly held within otherwise-orthodox evangelicalism. John MacArthur publicly held a version of this in the 1980s and publicly retracted it in 1999, accepting eternal Sonship. The MacArthur-style position is heterodox-not-cult and recoverable when the holder still affirms the full deity and the pre-existence of the second person of the Trinity. The cult-marker bar applies to the hard form. Counter-witness across patristic, Catholic, Lutheran, Anglican, Reformed, Methodist, Anabaptist, Pentecostal, and Eastern Orthodox lineages is unanimous: the Son is eternally Son, begotten not made, of one substance with the Father. Nicaea 325 is the canonical patristic landmark.

stem_audit.notes: Statement uses creedal-shared vocabulary (eternally the Son, co-eternal and co-equal, not adopted or begun in time). It names no heretics and embeds no Reformed-distinctive theory of mechanism. The lexical claim (the Son's Sonship is not a temporal beginning) is testable directly against the apparatus. No neutralization needed.

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: yes, the Son has always been the Son. He did not become the Son at his birth in Bethlehem, or at his baptism, or at his resurrection. The strongest reason is John 1:1-2, which says in the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. The word was here is a continuous past tense. It does not mean the Word started; it means the Word was already there. Verse 14 then says this Word became flesh as Jesus, and verse 18 says he is in the bosom of the Father, an ongoing relationship. In John 8:58 Jesus says before Abraham came into being, I am. He uses a present tense for himself and a past tense for Abraham on purpose. In John 17:5 Jesus prays for the glory he had with the Father before the world existed. In Hebrews 1:2 the Father makes the worlds through the Son, so the Son cannot be one of the made things. The honest counterargument is Hebrews 1:5, which quotes Psalm 2:7, you are my Son, today I have begotten you. Critics ask, if there was a today on which he became Son, was he always the Son. The fair answer is that Psalm 2 is a coronation psalm. It is sung at the installation of the king. Hebrews applies it to the Son's installation as the messianic king at his resurrection, not to the start of his being the Son. A second complicating text is Colossians 1:15, which calls the Son firstborn of all creation. The word firstborn in the Bible means rank, not birth order. Psalm 89:27 says God will make David his firstborn, even though David was the youngest son of Jesse. The very next verses in Colossians 1:16-17 say all things were made through him and for him, so the firstborn one is not himself among the created. The verdict holds without forcing the language. The honest acknowledgement is that a few otherwise-orthodox modern teachers have at times said the Son became Son only at the incarnation while still affirming his full deity from eternity. That is a smaller error than the heretical view and has been publicly corrected by at least one major teacher who held it. The hard denial that the Son had a beginning in being is what historic Christianity has called the line between Christianity and not-Christianity since the fourth century.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Agreement on eternal Sonship is unanimous across every tracked historic Christian lineage. The patristic fathers settled the question at the Council of Nicaea in 325, in response to Arius who taught there was a time when the Son was not. The Nicene Creed says the Son is begotten of the Father before all worlds, God of God, Light of Light, very God of very God, begotten not made, of one substance with the Father. Athanasius defended this at length against the Arians. The Athanasian Creed says the Son is of the Father alone, not made, not created, but begotten. The Catholic Church teaches eternal generation of the Son in its Catechism. Eastern Orthodoxy confesses the same in the Divine Liturgy. The Lutheran Augsburg Confession, the Anglican Thirty-Nine Articles, the Methodist Articles of Religion, the Reformed Westminster Confession, the Anabaptist Dordrecht Confession, and the Pentecostal Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths all confess the Son as eternally God of one substance with the Father. Public-record carriers of denial place themselves outside historic Christianity. The Jehovah's Witnesses say the Son was created by the Father as the archangel Michael before all other things. The Christadelphians say the Son began at the incarnation. Mormonism says the Son was spiritually begotten in a pre-mortal existence as the first of many spirit children of the Father. Iglesia ni Cristo says Christ is a mere man chosen by God. Historic Arianism is the prototype that all created-Son views go back to. A separate and milder form of denial appeared within otherwise-orthodox modern evangelicalism in the form of incarnational Sonship, where Sonship is said to begin at the incarnation while the second person of the Trinity is still affirmed as fully and eternally God. John MacArthur publicly held this position and publicly retracted it in 1999. This milder form is heterodox by historic standards but is recoverable, and the recovery has happened on the public record.

scripture.key_term: λόγος logos G3056; θεός theos G2316; ἦν en G1510 (imperfect of eimi); ἀρχή arche G746
scripture.force: In the beginning the Word already was (imperfect ἦν, continuous past). The Word was with God and the Word was God. Verse 2 repeats: this one was in the beginning with God. The imperfect tense locates the Logos before the arche of creation itself. The Logos does not begin; the Logos is already present at the beginning.

scripture.key_term: λόγος logos G3056; σάρξ sarx G4561; μονογενοῦς monogenous G3439; παρὰ πατρός para patros G3962
scripture.force: The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory, glory as of the only-begotten from the Father. Monogenes (G3439) is from monos plus genos (one of a kind); Heb 11:17 uses it of Isaac who was not Abraham's only son, so it denotes uniqueness of relation, not chronological birth. Para patros names the Father as the source of the Son's glory, a continuous relational identification.

scripture.key_term: μονογενὴς θεός monogenes theos G3439 G2316 (NA28/UBS5 reading); ὁ ὢν ho on G1510 (present participle of eimi); κόλπος kolpos G2859; πατήρ pater G3962
scripture.force: No one has ever seen God; the only-begotten God, the one who is in the bosom of the Father, he has made him known. The NA28/UBS5 reading monogenes theos applies theos directly to the Son. The present participle ho on (the one being) describes an ongoing, unbroken state of intimate relation with the Father, not a state begun in time.

scripture.key_term: ἀγαπάω agapao G25; ἔδωκεν edoken G1325 (aorist of didomi); μονογενῆ monogene G3439; υἱός huios G5207
scripture.force: God so loved the world that he gave his only-begotten Son. The verb gave (aorist of didomi) takes as object an already-identified Son. The Father did not love the world and produce a Son to give; he gave the Son who already was. The grammar presupposes pre-existing Sonship.

scripture.key_term: πρίν prin G4250; γενέσθαι genesthai G1096 (aorist infinitive of ginomai, to come into being); ἐγὼ εἰμί ego eimi G1473 G1510 (present indicative of eimi)
scripture.force: Before Abraham came into being, I am. The verb tense contrast is deliberate. Abraham came into being (aorist of ginomai, bounded past origin). Jesus uses ego eimi (present indicative, continuous existence) of himself. The hearers reach for stones (v.59) because they understand this as a claim to divine, unoriginated existence. Verifies the Son existed before any creature.

scripture.key_term: δοξάζω doxazo G1392; δόξα doxa G1391; πρό pro G4253; κόσμος kosmos G2889; εἶναι einai G1510
scripture.force: Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world existed. The Son asks for restoration of a glory he possessed with the Father prior to creation. Possession of pre-creation glory with the Father in a Father-Son relation requires that the Son was Son before there was a world.

scripture.key_term: μορφή morphe G3444; θεός theos G2316; ὑπάρχων huparchon G5225 (present participle of huparcho); ἁρπαγμός harpagmos G725; κενόω kenoo G2758; δοῦλος doulos G1401
scripture.force: Christ Jesus, existing in the form of God (present participle huparchon, continuously subsisting), did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant. The participle huparchon denotes ongoing prior existence in the divine form, which the incarnation does not begin. The Son was already in the form of God before the kenosis.

scripture.key_term: εἰκών eikon G1504; πρωτότοκος prototokos G4416; κτίσις ktisis G2937; πρὸ πάντων pro panton G4253 G3956; συνέστηκεν sunesteken G4921 (perfect of sunistemi)
scripture.force: He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation. For in him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible. He is before all things and in him all things hold together. Prototokos in canonical usage denotes rank not chronology (Ps 89:27 LXX: God names David, Jesse's youngest, as his firstborn). Verses 16-17 explicitly place him outside the set of created things: through him and for him all things were created, and he holds them in being. Pro panton he is before all things.

scripture.key_term: υἱός huios G5207; αἰών aion G165; ἐποίησεν epoiesen G4160 (aorist of poieo); ἀπαύγασμα apaugasma G541; δόξα doxa G1391; χαρακτήρ charakter G5481; ὑπόστασις hupostasis G5287
scripture.force: In these last days God has spoken to us by a Son, whom he appointed heir of all things, through whom also he made the worlds (dia hou epoiesen tous aionas). The Son is named the radiance of the Father's glory and the exact imprint of his being. The agent through whom God made the worlds cannot be among the made things. Apaugasma (radiance) and charakter (exact imprint) denote ontological continuity with the Father, not creation by the Father.

scripture.key_term: υἱός huios G5207; σήμερον semeron G4594; γεγέννηκα gegenneka G1080 (perfect of gennao); citation of Ps 2:7
scripture.force: You are my Son; today I have begotten you. Hebrews quotes Psalm 2:7. In its psalm context this is a royal coronation oracle for the Davidic king on the day of installation. Hebrews applies it to the Son's installation as messianic king and high priest, not to the origin of his being-Son. The same Hebrews 1 chapter already named the Son as agent of creation (v.2) and as one whose throne is forever (v.8 quoting Ps 45:6-7 and calling him God). The today of begetting is the today of enthronement, not the origin of eternal Sonship.

scripture.key_term: בֵּן ben H1121 (son); יָלַד yalad H3205 (begotten); הַיּוֹם hayom (today)
scripture.force: You are my son, today I have begotten you. In its original setting this is the coronation oracle for the Davidic king, the moment of formal installation as son of God in the covenantal-royal sense. Hebrews 1:5 and Acts 13:33 read this Christologically as the day of Christ's enthronement (the resurrection in Acts 13:33). It is a coronation text, not an origin-of-Sonship text.

scripture.key_term: קָנָה qanah H7069 (possessed/acquired/begot); רֵאשִׁית reshit H7225 (beginning); עוֹלָם olam H5769 (from everlasting); חוֹלָלְתִּי cholalti H2342 (brought forth, polel of chul)
scripture.force: The LORD possessed me at the beginning of his way, before his works of old. From everlasting I was set up. Used by Arius (and later JWs) reading personified Wisdom as a created Son via the rendering created me. Qanah H7069 in canonical usage carries possessed / acquired / begot, not necessarily created out of nothing; cf. Gen 4:1 where Eve uses qanah of begetting Cain. Standard reading: poetic personification of divine Wisdom (a wisdom figure, identified Christologically in 1 Cor 1:24 and Col 2:3). Complicates only on the contested Arian translation choice.

scripture.key_term: ζωή zoe G2222 (life); ἔχειν echein G2192 (to have); ἐν ἑαυτῷ en heauto (in himself); ἔδωκεν edoken G1325 (gave)
scripture.force: As the Father has life in himself, so he has granted the Son also to have life in himself. The grant of self-existent life to the Son in the eternal relation is a classical proof-text for eternal generation: the Son has life in himself by eternal gift of the Father, not by creation. The lexical content fits the eternal-generation reading without forcing it.

scripture.key_term: ἀγάπη agape G26; μονογενῆ monogene G3439; υἱός huios G5207; ἀπέσταλκεν apestalken G649 (perfect of apostello, has sent)
scripture.force: In this the love of God was manifested among us, that God has sent his only-begotten Son into the world. Same grammar as John 3:16. The perfect apestalken (has sent) takes as object an already-identified Son. The Son is sent into the world; the sending presupposes prior Sonship.

scripture.key_term: ἐγὼ καὶ ὁ πατὴρ ἕν ἐσμεν; ἕν hen G1520 (neuter one, one thing)
scripture.force: I and the Father are one. Neuter hen (one thing, one essence) not heis (one person). Personal distinction (I and the Father) plus shared essence. Coheres with the eternal Father-Son relation, not a temporal-only Sonship.

scripture.key_term: מֹצָאוֹתָיו motsa'otav H4163 (his goings forth); קֶדֶם qedem H6924 (ancient time); מִימֵי עוֹלָם yemei olam H5769 (days of eternity)
scripture.force: Out of Bethlehem one shall come forth to be ruler in Israel, whose goings forth (motsa'otav) are from of old, from the days of eternity (yemei olam). Olam H5769 in plural construct with yemei reaches the deepest temporal anchor in Hebrew vocabulary. The Messianic ruler's origins-goings-forth are placed in unbounded antiquity, not in a created beginning.

hermeneutics.notes: Grammatico-historical reading of the canonical lexical pattern is sufficient. No interpretive framework (covenantal, dispensational, new-covenant, progressive-covenantalism) produces a different verdict on eternal Sonship; this doctrine sits prior to every such framework. Analogia scripturae is heavily invoked because the verdict rests on the canon-wide pattern (John 1:1-2, 1:14, 1:18, 3:16, 8:58, 17:5, Phil 2:6, Col 1:15-17, Heb 1:2-3, Mic 5:2) rather than any single passage. Progressive revelation matters in that the OT names the Messiah's pre-temporal origins (Mic 5:2 yemei olam) without disclosing the full Father-Son shape of the eternal relation; the NT discloses what was always true. The two complicating texts (Heb 1:5 quoting Ps 2:7, and Prov 8:22-25) are handled lexically without overturning the pattern: Ps 2:7 is a coronation-enthronement oracle re-applied to the Son's installation as messianic king (Acts 13:33 names this as the resurrection), and Prov 8:22 turns on a contested translation of qanah, with the surrounding context (Wisdom as personification, before the foundations of the earth) and the cross-canonical Christological identification of Wisdom (1 Cor 1:24, Col 2:3) governing the reading.

counter_witness.anchor: Nicene Creed (325), CCEL Schaff Creeds of Christendom
counter_witness.key_phrase: We believe in one Lord Jesus Christ, the only-begotten Son of God, begotten of the Father before all worlds, God of God, Light of Light, very God of very God, begotten not made, of one substance with the Father.
The Son is of the Father alone: not made, nor created: but begotten. So there is one Father, not three Fathers; one Son, not three Sons.

counter_witness.anchor: Athanasius, Discourses Against the Arians (Contra Arianos), NPNF Series 2 Vol 4 (CCEL)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Son is eternally begotten of the Father, not made and not created. There was never when he was not. To say there was a time when the Son was not is to deny the Father is eternally Father.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church, paragraphs 240-262, on the Son of God
counter_witness.key_phrase: Jesus is the only-begotten Son of the Father, eternally begotten, consubstantial with the Father, true God from true God. The Father has begotten the Son from all eternity.

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession (1530), Article I, Of God; bookofconcord.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: There is one Divine Essence; yet there are three Persons, of the same essence and power, who also are coeternal, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost.

counter_witness.anchor: Thirty-Nine Articles of Religion (1571), Article II, Of the Word, or Son of God
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Son, which is the Word of the Father, begotten from everlasting of the Father, the very and eternal God, of one substance with the Father, took man's nature in the womb of the blessed Virgin.

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith (1646), Chapter 2, paragraph 3
counter_witness.key_phrase: In the unity of the Godhead there be three Persons of one substance, power, and eternity: God the Father, God the Son, and God the Holy Ghost. The Son is eternally begotten of the Father.

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion (Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles), Article II
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Son, who is the Word of the Father, the very and eternal God, of one substance with the Father, took man's nature in the womb of the blessed Virgin.

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession of Faith (1632), Article I-II, Of God and Of Christ
counter_witness.key_phrase: We believe in one eternal, almighty God, Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, three persons coequal in power, glory, majesty, and dominion. The Son is eternally of the Father.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God, Statement of Fundamental Truths, Article 2, The One True God; Article 3, The Deity of the Lord Jesus Christ
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Lord Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, possessed eternal existence with the Father, in distinction from but in essential and eternal oneness with the Father.

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, The Symbol of Faith: The Son of God
counter_witness.key_phrase: Begotten not made, of one essence with the Father. The Son is eternally generated from the Father; there was never a time when the Son did not exist.

web.quote: John 1:1 imperfect en (G1510) appears three times in v.1 and once in v.2; continuous past existence. The Logos was already there before the arche of creation.
John 1:18: monogenes theos ho on eis ton kolpon tou patros. NA28/UBS5 reads monogenes theos. The present participle ho on names the Son as the one continuously being in the bosom of the Father.
John 8:58: prin Abraam genesthai ego eimi. Aorist ginomai of Abraham coming into being contrasted with present eimi of the Son. The hearers reach for stones, understanding a divine self-claim.
John 17:5: doxason me te doxe he eichon pro tou ton kosmon einai para soi. The Son had glory with the Father before the world existed; pre-creation Father-Son relation.
Phil 2:6: en morphe theou huparchon. Present participle of huparcho denotes continuous prior subsistence in the form of God. The Son was already in the form of God before the kenosis.
Heb 1:2: di hou kai epoiesen tous aionas. The Son is the agent through whom God made the worlds; the agent of creation cannot be among created things.
Col 1:15-17: prototokos (G4416) of all creation. Canonical usage (Ps 89:27 LXX, David named firstborn though youngest) denotes rank not chronology. Vv.16-17: all things created in/through/for him.
G3439 monogenes: one of a kind. Heb 11:17 uses it of Isaac, who was not Abraham's only son. So monogenes denotes uniqueness of relation, not chronological-only birth.
G4416 prototokos: literal firstborn but figuratively rank/preeminence. Ps 89:27 LXX names David, Jesse's youngest, as firstborn. Col 1:15 reads as preeminence over creation.
John 1:14: monogenous para patros. The only-begotten from the Father. Para with genitive names the Father as ongoing source of the Son's glory.
John 3:16: edoken ton huion ton monogene. The double article identifies a Son already known; God gave the Son who already was, not produced a Son to give.
Heb 1:5 quoting Ps 2:7: semeron gegenneka se. Ps 2 is a royal coronation oracle; Acts 13:33 reads the today as the resurrection-installation, not the origin of Sonship.
Prov 8:22: qanah (H7069) the LORD possessed me. Semantic range covers possessed / acquired / begot; not unambiguously created. Used by Arius on the contested rendering.
Orthodox Church in America: Begotten not made, of one essence with the Father. The Son is eternally generated; there was never a time when the Son did not exist.
Methodist Article II: The Son, who is the Word of the Father, the very and eternal God, of one substance with the Father.
Augsburg I: three Persons of the same essence and power, who also are coeternal, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost.
Athanasian Creed: The Son is of the Father alone: not made, nor created: but begotten. So there is one Father, one Son, one Holy Ghost.
